pattern: Every row is either a base pattern or its exact complement, so the whole grid reduces to two binary signatures whose feasibility and minimal swap count can be read off directly.
hints: A valid chessboard has only two kinds of rows: one pattern and its bitwise complement; the same is true of columns.
Check the corners-XOR consistency and that each row and column has a near-balanced count of ones, then the rows and columns can be solved independently.

For a dimension, compare its first line against the ideal alternating pattern, count mismatched positions, and for odd n the parity forces one target while for even n you take the cheaper of the two; each swap fixes two misplaced lines so divide the mismatch count by two.

edge_inputs.description: 4x4 needing one row swap and one column swap
impossible to ever form a chessboard
complementary chessboard with top-left one, still zero moves
all ones can never become a chessboard
3x3 valid alternating board needs no moves
rows are valid complements but columns are unbalanced and impossible

stress.description: build a genuine chessboard then apply random row and column swaps so a solution always exists
stress.note: guaranteed solvable boards via random swaps of a real chessboard

stress.description: fully random binary boards that are usually impossible, stressing the feasibility checks

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def movesToChessboard(self, board):
        n = len(board)
        # Two reference rows: a base and its complement must cover all rows.
        base = board[0]
        comp = [1 - v for v in base]
        for row in board:
            if row != base and row != comp:
                return -1
        basec = board[0]
        compc = [1 - v for v in basec]
        for j in range(n):
            col = [board[i][j] for i in range(n)]
            colbase = [board[i][0] for i in range(n)]
            colcomp = [1 - v for v in colbase]
            if col != colbase and col != colcomp:
                return -1
        # Balance checks.
        rowsum = sum(board[0])
        if not (n // 2 <= rowsum <= (n + 1) // 2):
            return -1
        colsum = sum(board[i][0] for i in range(n))
        if not (n // 2 <= colsum <= (n + 1) // 2):
            return -1
        # Count rows equal to base; complement count is the rest.
        rowEqualBase = sum(1 for row in board if row == base)
        colEqualBase = sum(1 for i in range(n) if [board[i][k] for k in range(n)][0] == board[0][0] and all(board[i][k] == board[0][k] for k in range(n)))
        def cost(line, n):
            # mismatches against pattern starting with 0
            m0 = sum(1 for i, v in enumerate(line) if v != i % 2)
            if n % 2 == 0:
                m1 = n - m0
                return min(m0, m1) // 2
            else:
                # parity forces a specific target
                if m0 % 2 == 0:
                    return m0 // 2
                return (n - m0) // 2
        rowMoves = cost(board[0], n)
        colMoves = cost([board[i][0] for i in range(n)], n)
        return rowMoves + colMoves
